Critique of the Problem Formulation section. The section gestures at a formalization but never actually formalizes anything; it also buries the contribution and conflates setup with method.

Major issues

1. No actual formalism. The section claims to "formalize" the problem but contains no equations, no decision variables defined symbolically, no state/action/transition specification, no constraint expressions. Readers see prose names ("quality-of-service penalty," "commitment penalty," "aggregate transition equations") with no mathematical objects. For a problem-formulation section this is the core deficiency. At minimum, you need: state s_t, action a_t, transition, per-step cost c_t, constraint set, and the objective J written out.

2. Undefined symbols introduced inline. N, T, p(t), c(t), d(t), K, M, O(NT) all appear without a notation block, and quantities such as the buy-sell spread, the QoS penalty, and the commitment penalty are named but not defined. The bounds on c(t), d(t) are mentioned but not given.

3. Contribution paragraph is misplaced. The fourth paragraph announces the central contribution (unified evaluation framework, deployment-gap metric, commitment reliability frontier). This belongs in the introduction, not in problem formulation. Worse, it forward-references two artifacts (deployment-gap, reliability frontier) that this section never defines and that a reader cannot evaluate here.

4. Two-level architecture is a method choice, not a problem statement. The "aggregate planner + vehicle-level dispatcher" decomposition and the statement that "strategies differ only in how p(t) is computed" describe your solution scaffolding. Move to a Methods/Architecture section, or clearly mark this paragraph as scoping the solution space being compared.

5. Aggregate state representation is also methodology. The K SOC bins / M remaining-time bins paragraph is a representational choice for the planner, not part of the problem definition. The O(NT) complexity remark is also out of place — individual tracking is O(N) per step, and the comparison to the binned representation only makes sense once you have introduced the planner.

6. Unsupported or vague claims. "Energy settlement is asymmetric, with the buy-sell spread common to all three markets in our evaluation" — the three markets are not introduced or cited; the reader does not know which markets or what "common spread" means quantitatively. "Standard transition equations with arrivals and departures" — give them, or cite a specific source; "standard" is doing too much work.

7. Weights "chosen either fixed a priori or learned during training" mixes two regimes without saying which strategies use which, undermining the claim that strategies differ only in p(t) computation. If learned weights are part of one strategy, the objective is not actually shared across strategies.

Suggested restructuring

- Para 1: Setting and notation (N, T, Δt = 30 min, define fleet, vehicle index i, SOC, arrivals/departures).
- Para 2: State, action, transition (write them).
- Para 3: Per-step cost decomposition (energy with asymmetric prices, QoS penalty, commitment penalty — each defined).
- Para 4: Constraints (per-vehicle SOC bounds, charge/discharge bounds, commitment intervals).
- Para 5: Objective and decision problem (write J explicitly; state whether weights are fixed or part of the policy).
- Move the two-level decomposition, the binned representation, and the contribution statement out of this section.

Smaller prose points

- "dual constraints" in the opening is vague; name them (QoS and commitment) on first mention.
- "decomposed as p(t) = c(t) − d(t) with bounded charging and discharging powers" — give the bounds and state whether c(t)·d(t) = 0 is enforced (simultaneous charge/discharge).
- "Aggregate dynamics follow standard transition equations" should either include the equations or be deleted.

No external claims to verify; all comments are about internal structure and completeness of the section as written.
